channel.title: Projects on Shawn M Graham
channel.description: Recent content in Projects on Shawn M Graham

item.title: Glitch
item.description: In my open notebook I’ve been thinking of much the same thing in terms of just sound, calling it ‘soundbashing history’. I think I’ll go with ‘Glitch’ right now as a more comprehensive idea. The way things break is interesting. Consider – when I have students beginning DH work, the terminal or command line is a scary place. Type something wrong, and the screen fills with error messages. Parse those errors correctly, and you learn much.

item.title: ODATE: Open Digital Archaeology Textbook Environment
item.description: NOW PUBLISHED IN PERPETUAL BETA - o-date.github.io Digital archaeology as a subfield rests upon the creative use of primarily open-source and/or open-access materials to archive, reuse, visualize, analyze and communicate archaeological data. Digital archaeology encourages innovative and critical use of open access data and the development of digital tools that facilitate linkages and analysis across varied digital sources. To that end, the proposed ‘e-textbook’ is an integrated cloud-based digital exploratory laboratory of multiple cloud-computing tools with teaching materials that instructors will be able to use ‘out-of-the-box’ with a single click, or to remix as circumstances dictate.

item.title: Archaeogaming
item.description: A series of experiments involving making games, studying games, and teaching with/about/in games. Primarily video, but not necessarily so.

item.title: Epoiesen: A journal for creative engagement in history and archaeology
item.description: ἐποίησεν (epoiesen)- made – is a journal for exploring creative engagement with the past, especially through digital means. It publishes primarily what might be thought of as ‘paradata’ or artist’s statements that accompany playful and unfamiliar forms of singing the past into existence. These could be visualizations, art works, games, pop-up installations, poetry, hypertext fiction, procedurally generated works, or other forms yet to be devised. We seek to document and valorize the scholarly creativity that underpins our representations of the past.

item.title: The Bone Trade
item.description: There is a thriving online trade in anatomical, ethnographic and archaeological human remains that makes ready use of new social media such as Instagram, Facebook, Etsy, and until recently, eBay. The “fetishization” of the ‘exotic’ dead that underpins this trade by its very nature transforms pieces of the body into material culture: curios, commodities or objets d’art. This practice has deep Colonial-era roots, but today’s e-commerce and social media platforms have only expanded collectors’ reach and made participation open to anyone with interest and spare finances.
